questions.prompt: Let \(f(x)=x^3-3x^2-4x+12\), and define \[h(x)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{f(x)}{x-3},&x\ne3,\p,&x=3.\end{cases}\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find all zeros of \(f\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a valid factorization step.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a valid factorization step.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find \(p\) so that \(h\) is continuous at \(x=3\). Justify.
parts.answer: For \(x\ne3\), \[h(x)=\frac{(x-3)(x-2)(x+2)}{x-3}=x^2-4.\]Thus \[\lim_{x\to3}h(x)=9-4=5.\]Continuity requires \[\boxed{p=5}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for cancellation away from x=3.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for cancellation away from x=3.

rubric.description: 1 point for p and continuity justification.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for p and continuity justification.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Using this value of \(p\), determine whether \(h\) is even.
parts.answer: With \(p=5\), the piecewise definition agrees with \(h(x)=x^2-4\) at every real number, including \(x=3\). Therefore \[h(-x)=(-x)^2-4=h(x),\]so \(\boxed{h\text{ is even}}\).

rubric.description: 1 point for recognizing the global formula.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for recognizing the global formula.

questions.prompt: Let \(R\) be the region bounded by \[y=\frac4x\qquad\text{and}\qquad y=(x-3)^2.\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the area of \(R\).
parts.answer: The curves intersect at \(x=1\) and \(x=4\). On this interval, \(4/x\) is above \((x-3)^2\). Therefore \[A=\int_1^4\left(\frac4x-(x-3)^2\right)dx=4\ln4-3=\boxed{\ln256-3}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the volume when \(R\) is revolved about the \(x\)-axis.
parts.answer: Using washers, \[V=\pi\int_1^4\left[\left(\frac4x\right)^2-(x-3)^4\right]dx.\]Evaluation gives \[\boxed{V=\frac{27\pi}{5}}.\]

questions.prompt: Consider the hyperbola \[3x^2-y^2=23.\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: A point moves on \(3x^2-y^2=23\), with \(dy/dt=4\). Find \(dx/dt\) when \(x=4\) on the upper branch.
parts.answer: Differentiate with respect to time: \[6x\frac{dx}{dt}-2y\frac{dy}{dt}=0.\]At \(x=4\), the upper branch has \(y=5\). Hence \[24\frac{dx}{dt}-40=0,\qquad \boxed{\frac{dx}{dt}=\frac53}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: For what values of \(k\) is \(2x+9y+k=0\) normal to the hyperbola?
parts.answer: The line has slope \(-2/9\). For the hyperbola, \[y'=\frac{3x}{y},\qquad m_{\rm normal}=-\frac{y}{3x}.\]Set \(-y/(3x)=-2/9\), so \(y=2x/3\). Substitution gives \(x=\pm3\), \(y=\pm2\). Then \[k=-(2x+9y),\]so \[\boxed{k=-24\text{ or }k=24}.\]

questions.prompt: Analyze \[y=e^{\sin x}\qquad\text{for}\qquad \pi\le x\le2\pi.\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find all maximum and minimum points on the interval. Justify.
parts.answer: For \(y=e^{\sin x}\), \[y'=e^{\sin x}\cos x.\]On \([\pi,2\pi]\), the interior critical point is \(x=3\pi/2\), where \(y=e^{-1}\), the absolute minimum. The endpoints both have value 1 and are absolute maxima. Thus \[\boxed{(\pi,1),(2\pi,1)\text{ maxima};\quad(3\pi/2,e^{-1})\text{ minimum}.}\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Sketch the graph on the stated interval.
parts.answer: The graph begins at \((\pi,1)\), decreases smoothly to \((3\pi/2,e^{-1})\), and increases symmetrically to \((2\pi,1)\). It remains positive throughout.

rubric.description: 1 point for the minimum and monotonicity.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the minimum and monotonicity.

rubric.description: 1 point for a smooth positive graph.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a smooth positive graph.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Write an equation for the axis of symmetry.
parts.answer: Because \(\sin(3\pi/2+u)=\sin(3\pi/2-u)=-\cos u\), the graph is symmetric about \[\boxed{x=\frac{3\pi}{2}}.\]

questions.prompt: Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus in each part.

parts.label: (a)(i)
parts.prompt: Given \(5x^3+40=\int_c^x f(t)dt\), find \(f(x)\).
parts.answer: Differentiate both sides with respect to \(x\): \[15x^2=f(x).\]Therefore \[\boxed{f(x)=15x^2}.\]

parts.label: (a)(ii)
parts.prompt: Find the value of \(c\).
parts.answer: Set \(x=c\). The integral is zero, so \[5c^3+40=0\implies c^3=-8\implies\boxed{c=-2}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.answer: A variable lower limit contributes a negative sign: \[\boxed{F'(x)=-\sqrt{1+x^{16}}}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for recognizing the lower limit.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for recognizing the lower limit.

rubric.description: 1 point for substituting x for t.

questions.prompt: For differentiable \(f\), define \[f^*(x)=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x-h)}{2h}.\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find \(f^*(x)\) for \(f(x)=x^2+x\).
parts.answer: \[f(x+h)-f(x-h)=4xh+2h.\]Therefore \[f^*(x)=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{4xh+2h}{2h}=\boxed{2x+1}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find \(f^*(x)\) for \(f(x)=\cos x\).
parts.answer: Using \(\cos(x+h)-\cos(x-h)=-2\sin x\sin h\), \[f^*(x)=-\sin x\lim_{h\to0}\frac{\sin h}{h}=\boxed{-\sin x}.\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: State and justify the relationship between \(f^*\) and the usual derivative \(f'\).
parts.answer: For differentiable \(f\), \[\frac{f(x+h)-f(x-h)}{2h}=\frac12\left(\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}h+\frac{f(x)-f(x-h)}h\right).\]Each quotient tends to \(f'(x)\), so \[\boxed{f^*(x)=f'(x)}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for splitting the symmetric quotient.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for splitting the symmetric quotient.

rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying the forward derivative limit.

questions.prompt: Let \(f(x)=\cos x\) for \(0<x<2\pi\), and let \(g(x)=\ln x\) for \(x>0\). Let \(S\) be the composition of \(g\) with \(f\): \[S(x)=g(f(x))=\ln(\cos x).\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the domain of \(S\).
parts.answer: The logarithm requires \(\cos x>0\). Within \(0<x<2\pi\), this occurs on \[\boxed{\left(0,\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\cup\left(\frac{3\pi}{2},2\pi\right)}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for both intervals with open endpoints.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for both intervals with open endpoints.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the range of \(S\).
parts.answer: On the domain, \(0<\cos x<1\): the value \(1\) is approached near \(0\) and \(2\pi\) but is not attained, while \(\cos x\to0^+\) near the vertical boundaries. Therefore \[\boxed{\operatorname{Range}(S)=(-\infty,0)}.\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find all zeros of \(S\).
parts.answer: A zero would require \(\ln(\cos x)=0\), so \(\cos x=1\). In \([0,2\pi]\), that happens only at \(0\) and \(2\pi\), both excluded. Hence \[\boxed{S\text{ has no zeros}.}\]

rubric.description: 1 point for observing the candidate points are excluded.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for observing the candidate points are excluded.

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Find the slope of the tangent line to the graph of \(S\) at \(x=\frac{\pi}{3}\).
parts.answer: By the chain rule, \[S\'(x)=\frac{-\sin x}{\cos x}=-\tan x.\]Thus \[\boxed{S\'\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=-\sqrt3}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the logarithmic derivative structure.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the logarithmic derivative structure.

questions.prompt: Consider the function \[f(x)=(x^2-1)^3\]defined for all real numbers \(x\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: For what values of \(x\) is \(f\) increasing?
parts.answer: \[f\'(x)=6x(x^2-1)^2.\]Because \((x^2-1)^2\ge0\), the derivative is positive for \(x>0\) except for the isolated stationary point \(x=1\). The function is strictly increasing across that point, so \[\boxed{f\text{ is increasing on }(0,\infty)}.\]Equivalently, a derivative sign chart may list \((0,1)\cup(1,\infty)\) and then note that the monotonic behavior continues through \(x=1\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct sign analysis.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct sign analysis.

rubric.description: 1 point for the increasing interval(s) with correct interpretation of x=1.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the increasing interval(s) with correct interpretation of x=1.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the coordinates of all relative maximum and relative minimum points. Justify.
parts.answer: The derivative is negative for \(x<0\) and positive for \(x>0\), so the sign changes from negative to positive at \(x=0\). Thus \[\boxed{(0,-1)\text{ is a relative minimum}.}\]At \(x=\pm1\), the derivative is zero but does not change sign. Therefore \[\boxed{\text{there is no relative maximum}.}\]

rubric.description: 1 point for identifying the sign change at x=0.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying the sign change at x=0.

rubric.description: 1 point for the relative minimum coordinate.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the relative minimum coordinate.

rubric.description: 1 point for correctly rejecting x=±1 as extrema and stating no relative maximum.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for correctly rejecting x=±1 as extrema and stating no relative maximum.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: For what values of \(x\) is the graph of \(f\) concave upward?
parts.answer: \[f\'\'(x)=6(x^2-1)(5x^2-1).\]This product is positive when both factors have the same sign. Therefore the graph is concave upward on \[\boxed{(-\infty,-1)\cup\left(-\frac1{\sqrt5},\frac1{\sqrt5}\right)\cup(1,\infty)}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the critical second-derivative values.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the critical second-derivative values.

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Using the information from the previous parts, sketch the graph of \(f\).
parts.answer: The graph is even, has an absolute/relative minimum at \((0,-1)\), crosses the x-axis with horizontal tangents at \((\pm1,0)\), has inflection points at \(x=\pm1\) and \(x=\pm1/\sqrt5\), and rises to \(+\infty\) as \(x\to\pm\infty\).

rubric.description: 1 point for a sketch consistent with the symmetry, extrema, stationary crossings, concavity, and end behavior.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a sketch consistent with the symmetry, extrema, stationary crossings, concavity, and end behavior.

questions.prompt: Let \[f(x)=e^{x/2}.\]Let \(R\) be the region bounded by the line \(y=e\), the graph of \(f\), and the \(y\)-axis.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the area of \(R\).
parts.answer: The curves meet when \(e^{x/2}=e\), so \(x=2\). Hence \[A=\int_0^2\left(e-e^{x/2}\right)\,dx=\left[ex-2e^{x/2}\right]_0^2=\boxed{2}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the volume generated when \(R\) is revolved about the \(x\)-axis.
parts.answer: Using washers, the outer radius is \(e\) and the inner radius is \(e^{x/2}\): \[V=\pi\int_0^2\left(e^2-e^x\right)\,dx=\pi\left[ e^2x-e^x\right]_0^2=\boxed{\pi(e^2+1)}.\]

questions.prompt: A particle moves along the \(x\)-axis with acceleration \[a(t)=2t-10+\frac{12}{t},\qquad t>1.\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find an expression for the velocity \(v(t)\), given that \(v(1)=9\).
parts.answer: Integrating acceleration gives \[v(t)=t^2-10t+12\ln t+C.\]Using \(v(1)=9\) gives \(C=18\). Thus \[\boxed{v(t)=t^2-10t+12\ln t+18}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: For \(1<t<3\), at what value of \(t\) is the velocity a maximum? Justify.
parts.answer: Because \(v\'(t)=a(t)\), \[a(t)=\frac{2(t-2)(t-3)}{t}.\]On \((1,2)\), acceleration is positive; on \((2,3)\), it is negative. Therefore velocity changes from increasing to decreasing at \[\boxed{t=2}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for factoring or solving a(t)=0.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for factoring or solving a(t)=0.

rubric.description: 1 point for t=2 with maximum justification.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for t=2 with maximum justification.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find an expression for the position \(x(t)\), given that \(x(1)=-16\).
parts.answer: Integrating velocity, \[x(t)=\frac{t^3}{3}-5t^2+12t\ln t+6t+C.\]Since \(x(1)=-16\), \(C=-\frac{52}{3}\). Hence \[\boxed{x(t)=\frac{t^3}{3}-5t^2+12t\ln t+6t-\frac{52}{3}}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for integrating the polynomial terms.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for integrating the polynomial terms.

questions.prompt: A rectangle has constant area \(200\) square meters. Its length \(L\) is increasing at \(4\) meters per second.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the width \(W\) at the instant when the width is decreasing at \(0.5\) meter per second.
parts.answer: From \(LW=200\), \[L\frac{dW}{dt}+W\frac{dL}{dt}=0.\]At the stated instant, \(dW/dt=-\frac12\) and \(dL/dt=4\), so \(-\frac12L+4W=0\), or \(L=8W\). Combining this with \(LW=200\) gives \(8W^2=200\), so \[\boxed{W=5\text{ m}}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for differentiating with respect to time.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for differentiating with respect to time.

rubric.description: 1 point for relating L and W.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for relating L and W.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: At what rate is the diagonal \(D\) changing when \(W=10\) meters?
parts.answer: When \(W=10\), constant area gives \(L=20\). Also \[\frac{dW}{dt}=-\frac{W}{L}\frac{dL}{dt}=-2.\]Since \(D^2=L^2+W^2\), \[2D\frac{dD}{dt}=2L\frac{dL}{dt}+2W\frac{dW}{dt}.\]Here \(D=\sqrt{20^2+10^2}=10\sqrt5\), so \[\frac{dD}{dt}=\frac{20(4)+10(-2)}{10\sqrt5}=\boxed{\frac6{\sqrt5}=\frac{6\sqrt5}{5}\text{ m/s}}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the exact diagonal rate.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the exact diagonal rate.

questions.prompt: Let \[f(x)=\sin^3 x+\sin^3|x|.\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find \(f'(x)\) for \(x>0\).
parts.answer: For \(x>0\), \(|x|=x\), so \(f(x)=2\sin^3x\). Therefore \[\boxed{f\'(x)=6\sin^2x\cos x}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find \(f'(x)\) for \(x<0\).
parts.answer: For \(x<0\), \(|x|=-x\), and \(\sin|x|=\sin(-x)=-\sin x\). Hence \[f(x)=\sin^3x+(-\sin x)^3=0,\]so \[\boxed{f\'(x)=0}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for showing f(x)=0 on x<0.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for showing f(x)=0 on x<0.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Determine whether \(f\) is continuous at \(x=0\). Justify.
parts.answer: \(f(0)=0\). For \(x<0\), \(f(x)=0\), while for \(x>0\), \(f(x)=2\sin^3x\to0\). Thus both one-sided limits equal \(f(0)\), and \[\boxed{f\text{ is continuous at }0}.\]

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Determine whether \(f'(0)\) exists. Justify.
parts.answer: Using the definition, for \(h<0\), \(f(h)/h=0\). For \(h>0\), \[\frac{f(h)-f(0)}h=\frac{2\sin^3h}{h}=2\left(\frac{\sin h}{h}\right)\sin^2h\to0.\]The one-sided derivative limits agree, so \[\boxed{f\'(0)=0}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the right derivative limit.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the right derivative limit.

questions.prompt: Given the function \(f(x)=x^3-x^2-4x+4\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the zeros of \(f\).
parts.answer: Factor by grouping: \[f(x)=x^2(x-1)-4(x-1)=(x-1)(x-2)(x+2).\] Hence the zeros are \[\boxed{x=-2,\ 1,\ 2}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for a valid factorization step.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a valid factorization step.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write an equation of the line tangent to the graph of \(f\) at \(x=-1\).
parts.answer: \[f(-1)=6,\qquad f^{\prime}(x)=3x^2-2x-4,\qquad f^{\prime}(-1)=1.\] Therefore \[\boxed{y-6=x+1},\quad\text{or}\quad\boxed{y=x+7}.\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: The point \((a,b)\) is on the graph of \(f\), and the tangent line at \((a,b)\) passes through \((0,-8)\), which is not on the graph. Find \(a\) and \(b\).
parts.answer: The tangent at \(x=a\) gives \(-8=f(a)-af^{\prime}(a)\). Since \[f(a)-af^{\prime}(a)=-2a^3+a^2+4,\]we obtain \[2a^3-a^2-12=0=(a-2)(2a^2+3a+6).\]The quadratic has no real roots, so \(a=2\), and \(b=f(2)=0\). Thus \[\boxed{(a,b)=(2,0)}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for simplifying to the cubic condition.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for simplifying to the cubic condition.

rubric.description: 1 point for solving a=2 as the only real solution.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for solving a=2 as the only real solution.

questions.prompt: Let \(f(x)=(1-x)^2\) for all real \(x\), let \(g(x)=\ln x\) for \(x>0\), and let \(h(x)=(1-\ln x)^2\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Determine whether \(h=f\circ g\) or \(h=g\circ f\).
parts.answer: \[f(g(x))=(1-\ln x)^2=h(x).\] Therefore \[\boxed{h=f\circ g}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find \(h^{\prime}(x)\).
parts.answer: By the chain rule, \[h^{\prime}(x)=2(1-\ln x)\left(-\frac1x\right)=\boxed{\frac{2(\ln x-1)}{x}}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct simplified derivative.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct simplified derivative.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find \(h''(x)\).
parts.answer: Differentiate \(2(\ln x-1)x^{-1}\): \[h''(x)=2\left(\frac1{x^2}-\frac{\ln x-1}{x^2}\right)=\boxed{\frac{2(2-\ln x)}{x^2}}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for a valid product or quotient rule.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a valid product or quotient rule.

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Sketch the graph of \(h\), showing important features.
parts.answer: The domain is \(x>0\). The graph decreases on \((0,e)\), has absolute minimum \((e,0)\), and increases on \((e,\infty)\). It is concave up on \((0,e^2)\), concave down on \((e^2,\infty)\), and has an inflection point at \((e^2,1)\). Also \(h(x)\to\infty\) as \(x\to0^+\) and as \(x\to\infty\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the minimum and monotonic behavior.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the minimum and monotonic behavior.

rubric.description: 1 point for end behavior and concavity.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for end behavior and concavity.

questions.prompt: Given \[f(x)=\frac{2x-2}{x^2+x-2}.\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: For what values of \(x\) is \(f\) discontinuous?
parts.answer: \[x^2+x-2=(x-1)(x+2),\]so the denominator is zero at \(x=1\) and \(x=-2\). Thus \[\boxed{x=-2,\ 1}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: At each discontinuity, determine whether \(\lim_{x\to a}f(x)\) exists and give its value when it exists.
parts.answer: For \(x\ne1,-2\), \[f(x)=\frac{2(x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)}=\frac2{x+2}.\] Therefore \[\boxed{\lim_{x\to1}f(x)=\frac23}.\]At \(x=-2\), the one-sided limits are opposite infinities, so the two-sided limit does not exist.

rubric.description: 1 point for simplifying with the domain restriction.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for simplifying with the domain restriction.

rubric.description: 1 point for the limit 2/3 at x=1.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the limit 2/3 at x=1.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Write equations for every vertical and horizontal asymptote and justify.
parts.answer: The noncanceled denominator gives the vertical asymptote \[\boxed{x=-2}.\]Because \(2/(x+2)\to0\) as \(|x|\to\infty\), the horizontal asymptote is \[\boxed{y=0}.\]The point \(x=1\) is a removable hole, not an asymptote.

rubric.description: 1 point for distinguishing the hole from an asymptote.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for distinguishing the hole from an asymptote.

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: A rational function \(g(x)=\dfrac{a}{b+x}\) satisfies \(g(x)=f(x)\) wherever \(f\) is defined. Find \(a\) and \(b\).
parts.answer: Comparing \(g(x)=a/(x+b)\) with the simplified form \(2/(x+2)\) gives \[\boxed{a=2,\qquad b=2}.\]

questions.prompt: A particle moves on the \(x\)-axis with velocity \(v(t)=\sin(2t)\). At \(t=0\), the particle is at the origin.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: For \(0<t<\pi\), find all times when the particle is moving to the left.
parts.answer: The particle moves left when \(v(t)<0\). On \(0<t<\pi\), \(\sin(2t)<0\) exactly when \[\boxed{\frac\pi2<t<\pi}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct sine interval.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct sine interval.

rubric.description: 1 point for the final interval in t.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the final interval in t.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write an expression for the position \(s(t)\).
parts.answer: \[s(t)=s(0)+\int_0^t\sin(2u)\,du=\left[-\frac12\cos(2u)\right]_0^t.\]Thus \[\boxed{s(t)=\frac{1-\cos(2t)}2}.\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: For \(0<t<\frac\pi2\), find the average value of the position function.
parts.answer: The average value on \([0,\pi/2]\) is \[\frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{1-\cos(2t)}2\,dt=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[t-\frac12\sin(2t)\right]_0^{\pi/2}=\boxed{\frac12}.\]

questions.prompt: Given the curve \[x^2-xy+y^2=9.\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Write a general expression for the slope \(dy/dx\).
parts.answer: Differentiating implicitly gives \[2x-(xy^{\prime}+y)+2yy^{\prime}=0.\]Therefore \[(2y-x)y^{\prime}=y-2x,\qquad\boxed{y^{\prime}=\frac{y-2x}{2y-x}}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the coordinates of the points where the tangents are vertical.
parts.answer: Vertical tangents occur when \(2y-x=0\), provided the numerator is nonzero. Set \(x=2y\): \[4y^2-2y^2+y^2=9\Rightarrow 3y^2=9.\]Thus \[\boxed{(2\sqrt3,\sqrt3)\quad\text{and}\quad(-2\sqrt3,-\sqrt3)}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for substitution into the curve.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for substitution into the curve.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: At \((0,3)\), find the rate of change of the slope with respect to \(x\).
parts.answer: First, \(y^{\prime}(0,3)=1/2\). Differentiating \(2x-xy^{\prime}-y+2yy^{\prime}=0\) again gives \[(2y-x)y^{\prime\prime}+2-2y^{\prime}+2(y^{\prime})^2=0.\]At \((0,3)\), \[6y^{\prime\prime}+2-1+\frac12=0,\]so \[\boxed{y^{\prime\prime}=-\frac14}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for a correct second differentiation.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for a correct second differentiation.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the maximum area of a rectangle with two vertices on the \(x\)-axis and two vertices on the graph of \(f\). Justify.
parts.answer: Let the upper-right vertex be \((x,e^{-x^2})\), \(x>0\). Symmetry gives \[A(x)=2xe^{-x^2}.\]Then \[A^{\prime}(x)=2e^{-x^2}(1-2x^2),\]so the only positive critical point is \(x=1/\sqrt2\). The derivative changes from positive to negative there, so \[\boxed{A_{\max}=\sqrt{\frac2e}}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for x=1/√2 and a maximum justification.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for x=1/√2 and a maximum justification.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Let \(R\) be the first-quadrant region bounded by the axes, the graph of \(f\), and \(x=k\). Find the volume generated by revolving \(R\) about the \(y\)-axis.
parts.answer: Using cylindrical shells, \[V(k)=2\pi\int_0^k xe^{-x^2}\,dx=2\pi\left[-\frac12e^{-x^2}\right]_0^k.\]Thus \[\boxed{V(k)=\pi(1-e^{-k^2})}.\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Evaluate the limit of the volume as \(k\to\infty\).
parts.answer: Because \(e^{-k^2}\to0\), \[\boxed{\lim_{k\to\infty}V(k)=\pi}.\]

questions.prompt: Let \(g\) and \(h\) be twice-differentiable for all real \(x\), and suppose that for every \(x\): \[(g(x))^2+(h(x))^2=1,\qquad g^{\prime}(x)=(h(x))^2,\qquad h(x)>0,\qquad g(0)=0.\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Justify that \(h^{\prime}(x)=-g(x)h(x)\) for every \(x\).
parts.answer: Differentiate \(g^2+h^2=1\): \[2gg^{\prime}+2hh^{\prime}=0.\]Since \(g^{\prime}=h^2\) and \(h>0\), \[h^{\prime}=-\frac{gg^{\prime}}h=-\frac{gh^2}h=\boxed{-gh}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for using h>0 to simplify.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for using h>0 to simplify.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Justify that \(h\) has a relative maximum at \(x=0\).
parts.answer: From the identity and \(g(0)=0\), positivity gives \(h(0)=1\). Also \(h^{\prime}(0)=0\). Differentiating \(h^{\prime}=-gh\) gives \[h^{\prime\prime}=-g^{\prime}h-gh^{\prime}.\]Thus \(h^{\prime\prime}(0)=-(1)(1)-0=-1<0\), so \[\boxed{h\text{ has a relative maximum at }x=0}.\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Justify that the graph of \(g\) has a point of inflection at \(x=0\).
parts.answer: Because \(g^{\prime}=h^2>0\), \(g\) is strictly increasing. Since \(g(0)=0\), we have \(g(x)<0\) for \(x<0\) and \(g(x)>0\) for \(x>0\). Also \[g^{\prime\prime}=2hh^{\prime}=-2gh^2.\]Therefore \(g^{\prime\prime}>0\) to the left of 0 and \(g^{\prime\prime}<0\) to the right. Concavity changes at \(0\), so \[\boxed{(0,0)\text{ is an inflection point of }g}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the sign of g on each side of 0.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the sign of g on each side of 0.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find all zeros of \(f\).
parts.answer:
Because \(x=2\) is a zero, division gives
\[f(x)=(x-2)^2(2x+5).\]
Therefore the zeros are \[\boxed{x=2\text{ (double)},\qquad x=-\frac52}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for both zeros and multiplicity.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for both zeros and multiplicity.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write an equation of the line normal to the graph at \(x=0\).
parts.answer:
\[f(0)=20,\qquad f^{\prime}(x)=6x^2-6x-12,\qquad f^{\prime}(0)=-12.\]
The normal slope is \(1/12\). Thus
\[\boxed{y-20=\frac1{12}x}.\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find all points where the tangent line is horizontal.
parts.answer:
Set
\[f^{\prime}(x)=6(x-2)(x+1)=0.\]
Thus \(x=-1\) or \(x=2\). Since \(f(-1)=27\) and \(f(2)=0\), the points are
\[\boxed{(-1,27)\quad\text{and}\quad(2,0)}.\]

questions.prompt: Let \(f(x)=xe^{-2x}\) on the interval \(0\le x\le10\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find all values of x for which the graph is increasing and all values for which it is decreasing.
parts.answer:
\[f^{\prime}(x)=e^{-2x}(1-2x).\]
Thus the graph is increasing for \(0\le x<1/2\) and decreasing for \(1/2<x\le10\).

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Give the coordinates of all absolute maximum and minimum points. Justify.
parts.answer:
The only interior critical point is \(x=1/2\), where the derivative changes from positive to negative, so the absolute maximum point is
\[\boxed{\left(\frac12,\frac1{2e}\right)}.\]
At the endpoints, \(f(0)=0\) and \(f(10)=10e^{-20}>0\), so the absolute minimum point is
\[\boxed{(0,0)}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the sign-change maximum justification.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the sign-change maximum justification.

questions.prompt: Find the maximum volume of an open-top box made by cutting equal squares from the corners of an \(8\)-inch by \(15\)-inch sheet and folding up the sides. Justify your answer.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the maximum volume and justify.
parts.answer:
If each cut has side length \(x\), then
\[V(x)=x(8-2x)(15-2x),\qquad 0\le x\le4.\]
Differentiation gives
\[V^{\prime}(x)=12x^2-92x+120=4(3x-5)(x-6).\]
The only feasible interior critical point is \(x=5/3\). The derivative changes from positive to negative there, and the endpoint volumes are zero. Therefore
\[\boxed{V_{\max}=V\left(\frac53\right)=\frac{2450}{27}\approx90.741\text{ in}^3}.\]

rubric.description: 2 points for the volume model and feasible domain.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 2 points for the volume model and feasible domain.

rubric.description: 2 points for solving the critical equation and rejecting x=6.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 2 points for solving the critical equation and rejecting x=6.

rubric.description: 2 points for a valid absolute-maximum justification.

questions.prompt: A particle moves along the \(x\)-axis with position \(x(t)=2\pi t+\cos(2\pi t)\) for \(0\le t\le3\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the velocity at time t.
parts.answer: \[\boxed{v(t)=2\pi\bigl(1-\sin(2\pi t)\bigr)}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for differentiating the cosine term.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for differentiating the cosine term.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the acceleration at time t.
parts.answer: \[\boxed{a(t)=-4\pi^2\cos(2\pi t)}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct coefficient and sign.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct coefficient and sign.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find all values of t, 0≤t≤3, for which the particle is at rest.
parts.answer:
Set \(v(t)=0\), so \(\sin(2\pi t)=1\). Therefore
\[\boxed{t=\frac14,\ \frac54,\ \frac94}.\]

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Find the maximum velocity.
parts.answer:
Since the minimum possible value of \(\sin(2\pi t)\) is \(-1\),
\[\boxed{v_{\max}=2\pi(1-(-1))=4\pi}.\]
It is attained at \(t=3/4,7/4,11/4\).

rubric.description: 1 point for identifying where it is attained.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for identifying where it is attained.

questions.prompt: Let \(R\) be the region bounded by \(y=\dfrac{\ln x}{x}\), the \(x\)-axis, and the line \(x=e\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the area of \(R\).
parts.answer:
The curve meets the \(x\)-axis at \(x=1\). Therefore
\[A=\int_1^e\frac{\ln x}{x}\,dx
=\left[\frac12(\ln x)^2\right]_1^e
=\boxed{\frac12}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the lower bound x=1.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the lower bound x=1.

rubric.description: 1 point for the substitution u=ln x.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the substitution u=ln x.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the volume generated when \(R\) is revolved about the \(y\)-axis.
parts.answer:
Using cylindrical shells,
\[V=2\pi\int_1^e x\left(\frac{\ln x}{x}\right)dx
=2\pi\int_1^e\ln x\,dx.\]
Since \(\int\ln x\,dx=x\ln x-x\),
\[V=2\pi[(x\ln x-x)]_1^e=\boxed{2\pi}.\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Sketch the graph of \(y=|f(x)|\).
parts.answer:
The portion of \(f\) below the \(x\)-axis on \(0<x<2\) is reflected upward. Equivalently,
\[|f(x)|=\begin{cases}x^2-2x,&x\le0\text{ or }x\ge2,\\-x^2+2x,&0<x<2.\end{cases}\]

rubric.description: 1 point for reflecting the negative part.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for reflecting the negative part.

rubric.description: 1 point for zeros and overall shape.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for zeros and overall shape.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Does \(\dfrac{d}{dx}|f(x)|\) exist at \(x=0\)? Justify.
parts.answer:
Near zero, the left branch is \(x^2-2x\), with left derivative \(-2\), while the right branch is \(-x^2+2x\), with right derivative \(2\). Since these are unequal,
\[\boxed{\frac{d}{dx}|f(x)|\text{ does not exist at }0}.\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Sketch the graph of \(y=f(|x|)\).
parts.answer:
Replacing \(x\) by \(|x|\) copies the right half of the graph across the \(y\)-axis:
\[\boxed{f(|x|)=x^2-2|x|}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for using the right half.

parts.prompt: Is \(f(|x|)\) continuous at \(x=0\)? Justify.
parts.answer:
Because
\[\lim_{x\to0}(x^2-2|x|)=0=f(0),\]
the function is \(\boxed{\text{continuous at }x=0}\).

questions.prompt: Let \(f(x)=x^3+ax^2+bx+c\). The graph has an inflection point at \((0,-2)\), and the average value of \(f\) on \([0,2]\) is \(-3\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Determine \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\).
parts.answer:
The point condition gives \(c=-2\). Since
\[f^{\prime\prime}(x)=6x+2a\]
and the inflection occurs at \(x=0\), \(a=0\). Hence \(f(x)=x^3+bx-2\). The average value condition gives
\[-3=\frac12\int_0^2(x^3+bx-2)\,dx=\frac12(4+2b-4)=b.\]
Thus
\[\boxed{a=0,\quad b=-3,\quad c=-2}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for setting up the average value.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for setting up the average value.

rubric.description: 1 point for simplifying the average value to b.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for simplifying the average value to b.

rubric.description: 1 point for b=-3 and all coefficients.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for b=-3 and all coefficients.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find all values of \(x\) in \((0,3)\) guaranteed by the Mean Value Theorem for \(f\) on \([0,3]\).
parts.answer:
The secant slope is
\[\frac{f(3)-f(0)}{3}=\frac{16-(-2)}3=6.\]
Set \(f^{\prime}(x)=3x^2-3=6\):
\[3x^2=9\Rightarrow x=\pm\sqrt3.\]
Only the value in \((0,3)\) is
\[\boxed{x=\sqrt3}.\]

questions.prompt: Let \(R\) be the region in the first quadrant bounded by \(y=x^3\) and \(y=\sqrt{x}\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the area of \(R\).
parts.answer:
The curves meet at \(x=0\) and \(x=1\). On this interval, \(\sqrt{x}\ge x^3\). Therefore
\[A=\int_0^1(\sqrt{x}-x^3)dx
=\left[\frac23x^{3/2}-\frac14x^4\right]_0^1
=\boxed{\frac5{12}}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the volume generated when \(R\) is revolved about the \(x\)-axis.
parts.answer:
Using washers,
\[V=\pi\int_0^1\left[(\sqrt{x})^2-(x^3)^2\right]dx
=\pi\int_0^1(x-x^6)dx.\]
Thus
\[V=\pi\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{x^7}{7}\right]_0^1
=\boxed{\frac{5\pi}{14}}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for outer and inner radii.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for outer and inner radii.

questions.prompt: A rectangle with sides parallel to the coordinate axes is inscribed under the parabola \(y=-4x^2+4\), symmetric about the \(y\)-axis. Its upper-right vertex is \(C=(x,y)\), where \(0<x<1\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the coordinates of \(C\) when the rectangle has maximum area.
parts.answer:
The rectangle has width \(2x\) and height \(4-4x^2\), so
\[A(x)=2x(4-4x^2)=8x-8x^3.\]
Then \(A^{\prime}(x)=8-24x^2\), giving \(x=1/\sqrt3\). This is a maximum because \(A^{\prime}\) changes from positive to negative. Hence
\[\boxed{C=\left(\frac1{\sqrt3},\frac83\right)}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Suppose \(x\) increases at \(2\) units per second. Find \(dA/dt\) when \(x=1/2\).
parts.answer:
\[\frac{dA}{dt}=(8-24x^2)\frac{dx}{dt}.\]
At \(x=1/2\) and \(dx/dt=2\),
\[\frac{dA}{dt}=(8-6)(2)=\boxed{4\text{ square units per second}}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for differentiating A with respect to time.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for differentiating A with respect to time.

questions.prompt: Let \(f(x)=\ln(x^2)\) for \(x>0\), and let \(g(x)=e^{2x}\) for \(x\ge0\). Define \(H=f\circ g\) and \(K=g\circ f\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the domain and a simplified formula for \(H\).
parts.answer:
Since \(g\) is defined for \(x\ge0\) and always produces a positive output,
\[H(x)=\ln\bigl((e^{2x})^2\bigr)=\ln(e^{4x})=4x.\]
Thus \[\boxed{\operatorname{Dom}H=[0,\infty),\qquad H(x)=4x}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the domain and a simplified formula for \(K\).
parts.answer:
For \(g(f(x))\), the input to \(g\) must satisfy \(f(x)\ge0\):
\[\ln(x^2)\ge0\Longleftrightarrow x^2\ge1.\]
Because the original domain of \(f\) is \(x>0\), this gives \(x\ge1\). Then
\[K(x)=e^{2\ln(x^2)}=e^{\ln(x^4)}=x^4.\]
Therefore \[\boxed{\operatorname{Dom}K=[1,\infty),\qquad K(x)=x^4}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for x≥1 after the domain restriction.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for x≥1 after the domain restriction.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find \(f^{-1}\), including its domain.
parts.answer:
Let \(y=\ln(x^2)=2\ln x\) with \(x>0\). Solving for \(x\) gives
\[x=e^{y/2}.\]
Thus
\[\boxed{f^{-1}(x)=e^{x/2}},\qquad \boxed{\operatorname{Dom}(f^{-1})=\mathbb R}.\]

questions.prompt: A particle moves along a line with acceleration \(a(t)=10e^{2t}\), initial velocity \(v(0)=5\), and initial position \(s(0)=0\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the velocity function.
parts.answer:
Integrating,
\[v(t)=5e^{2t}+C.\]
Since \(v(0)=5\), \(C=0\). Hence
\[\boxed{v(t)=5e^{2t}}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the distance traveled while the velocity increases from \(5\) to \(15\).
parts.answer:
Velocity reaches 15 when \(5e^{2t}=15\), so \(t=\frac12\ln3\). Since velocity is positive, distance equals displacement:
\[\int_0^{(1/2)\ln3}5e^{2t}dt
=\frac52[e^{2t}]_0^{(1/2)\ln3}
=\frac52(3-1)=\boxed{5}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the time when v=15.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the time when v=15.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find the position function.
parts.answer:
Integrating velocity,
\[s(t)=\frac52e^{2t}+C.\]
Using \(s(0)=0\) gives \(C=-5/2\). Therefore
\[\boxed{s(t)=\frac52\left(e^{2t}-1\right)}.\]

questions.prompt: Let \(f(x)=\cos x-\cos^2x\) on \(-\pi\le x\le\pi\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the \(x\)-intercepts.
parts.answer:
\[f(x)=\cos x(1-\cos x)=0.\]
On the interval, this gives
\[\boxed{x=-\frac\pi2,\ 0,\ \frac\pi2}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for factoring or equivalent reasoning.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for factoring or equivalent reasoning.

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find all relative maxima.
parts.answer:
\[f^{\prime}(x)=\sin x(2\cos x-1).\]
The derivative changes from positive to negative at \(x=\pm\pi/3\). Since \(f(\pm\pi/3)=1/4\), the relative maxima are
\[\boxed{\left(-\frac\pi3,\frac14\right),\quad\left(\frac\pi3,\frac14\right)}.\]

rubric.description: 1 point for the critical points and sign change.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the critical points and sign change.

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Find the intervals on which \(f\) is increasing.
parts.answer:
A sign chart for \(\sin x(2\cos x-1)\) gives
\[\boxed{(-\pi,-\pi/3)\cup(0,\pi/3)}.\]

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Sketch the graph, showing the intercepts and relative maxima.
parts.answer: The graph passes through the three intercepts, has the two relative maxima found above, is even, and reaches \(-2\) at both endpoints \(x=\pm\pi\).

rubric.description: 1 point for the correct overall shape and endpoint values.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the correct overall shape and endpoint values.

questions.prompt:
A continuous function \(f\) satisfies
\[x^4-5x^2(f(x))^2+4(f(x))^4=0\]
for every real \(x\), and its graph contains \((2,1)\) and \((-2,-2)\).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find a general expression for \(dy/dx\) on the implicit curve.
parts.answer:
Differentiating
\[x^4-5x^2y^2+4y^4=0\]
gives
\[4x^3-10xy^2-10x^2yy^{\prime}+16y^3y^{\prime}=0.\]
Therefore
\[\boxed{y^{\prime}=\frac{2x^3-5xy^2}{5x^2y-8y^3}}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Write the tangent line at \((2,1)\).
parts.answer:
Substitution gives
\[y^{\prime}=\frac{16-10}{20-8}=\frac12.\]
Hence
\[\boxed{y-1=\frac12(x-2)},\qquad\boxed{y=\frac x2}.\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Show that the point \((-2,-1)\) lies on this tangent line but not on the graph of \(f\).
parts.answer:
The line \(y=x/2\) contains \((-2,-1)\). However,
\[(-2)^4-5(-2)^2(-1)^2+4(-1)^4=16-20+4=0,\]
so it lies on the full implicit relation. The branch condition is the key: the stated graph has \(f(-2)=-2\), not \(-1\). Therefore \((-2,-1)\) is not on the graph of the selected function \(f\).

rubric.description: 1 point for checking the tangent line.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for checking the tangent line.

rubric.description: 1 point for distinguishing the implicit relation from the selected function branch.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for distinguishing the implicit relation from the selected function branch.

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Determine a formula for the continuous function \(f\).
parts.answer:
The relation factors as
\[(y^2-x^2)(4y^2-x^2)=0,\]
so possible lines are \(y=\pm x\) and \(y=\pm x/2\). The point \((2,1)\) selects \(y=x/2\) for \(x>0\), while \((-2,-2)\) selects \(y=x\) for \(x<0\). Both meet at the origin, so continuity gives
\[\boxed{f(x)=\begin{cases}x,&x<0,\x/2,&x\ge0.\end{cases}}\]

rubric.description: 1 point for selecting the branches from the two points.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for selecting the branches from the two points.

rubric.description: 1 point for the continuous piecewise formula.
rubric.evidenceRequired: 1 point for the continuous piecewise formula.

questions.prompt:
Define
\[f(x)=\begin{cases}
1+2p(x-1)+(x-1)^2,&x\le1,\\
qx+p,&x>1.
\end{cases}\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the relation between \(p\) and \(q\) required for continuity at \(x=1\).
parts.answer:
The left value is \(f(1)=1\). The right-hand limit is \(q+p\). Continuity requires
\[\boxed{p+q=1},\qquad\text{equivalently }\boxed{q=1-p}.\]

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find \(p\) and \(q\) so that \(f\) is differentiable at \(x=1\).
parts.answer:
The left derivative at 1 is \(2p\), while the right derivative is \(q\). Therefore differentiability requires \(q=2p\). Together with \(p+q=1\),
\[3p=1,\]
so
\[\boxed{p=\frac13,\qquad q=\frac23}.\]

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: For these values, is \(f^{\prime\prime}\) continuous on all real numbers? Explain.
parts.answer:
For \(x<1\), \(f^{\prime\prime}(x)=2\). For \(x>1\), \(f^{\prime\prime}(x)=0\). The first derivative is continuous at 1, but its one-sided slopes do not agree, so \(f^{\prime\prime}(1)\) does not exist. Hence
\[\boxed{f^{\prime\prime}\text{ is not continuous on }\mathbb R}.\]
Historical scoring sometimes treated “continuous on its domain” separately; the modern precise statement is that \(f^{\prime\prime}\) is undefined at the join.

questions.prompt: If \(f(x)=\dfrac1{1-x^2}\) has domain \(\{x:|x|>1\}\), what is its range?

questions.prompt: The graph of \(y=f'(x)\) is an upward-opening curve crossing the \(x\)-axis at \(x=-1\) and \(x=1\), and lying below the axis between them. Which graph could be \(f\)?

media.alt: Upward-opening derivative graph crossing the x-axis at negative one and positive one.

descriptor.ariaLabel: Upward-opening derivative graph crossing the x-axis at negative one and positive one.

questions.prompt: The area in the first quadrant enclosed by \(y=x^3+8\) and \(y=x+8\) is
questions.explanation: Intersections satisfy \(x^3=x\), so in the first quadrant the relevant endpoints are \(x=0\) and \(x=1\). The line lies above the cubic, so area \(=\int_0^1[(x+8)-(x^3+8)]dx=\int_0^1(x-x^3)dx=1/4\).

questions.prompt: If a continuous function \(f\) on all real numbers has maximum value 5 and minimum value \(-7\), which statements must be true? I. The maximum of \(f(|x|)\) is 5. II. The maximum of \(|f(x)|\) is 7. III. The minimum of \(f(|x|)\) is 0.
questions.explanation: Because the range of \(f\) lies in \([-7,5]\) and both endpoints occur, \(|f|\) has maximum 7, so II must hold. The function \(f(|x|)\) samples only nonnegative inputs, so its maximum need not be 5 and its minimum need not be 0. Thus only II is guaranteed.
questions.workedSolution: Because the range of \(f\) lies in \([-7,5]\) and both endpoints occur, \(|f|\) has maximum 7, so II must hold. The function \(f(|x|)\) samples only nonnegative inputs, so its maximum need not be 5 and its minimum need not be 0. Thus only II is guaranteed.
questions.noncalculatorSolution: Because the range of \(f\) lies in \([-7,5]\) and both endpoints occur, \(|f|\) has maximum 7, so II must hold. The function \(f(|x|)\) samples only nonnegative inputs, so its maximum need not be 5 and its minimum need not be 0. Thus only II is guaranteed.

commonMistakes: Confusing \(f(|x|)\) with \(|f(x)|\).
Assuming a global extremum occurs at a nonnegative input.
Using continuity to claim \(f(|x|)\) must cross zero even though only half the domain is sampled.
